sentences.text: Rena Gelissen, R-E-N-A, G-E-L-I-S-S-E-N.
At birth-- the last name, Kornreich, K-O-R-N-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] K-O-R-N-R-E-I-C-H.
Can you see I'm already nervous?
My birthday is August 24, 1920.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
I'm today 75 years old in a couple of months.
I was born in Tylicz, a little town, a little-- yeah, it's a little town it was called-- in the Carpathian mountains in the south of Poland.
And Tylicz is spelled T-Y-L-I-C-Z, Tylicz.
I was-- there were living, after World War I some Ruthenians.
Encyclopedia calls them Ruthenians, so I call them, too.
They were not exactly Russians.
They weren't Ukrainians.
They called them Ruthenians, so I call them, too.
They spoke the Russian language, and they used the Russian alphabet for their, for writing, OK?
And then they were-- all, all together there were 5,000 people in Tylicz.
And the majority was Polish Catholic, and the other one was Greek Orthodox, the Russians.
And there were 25 Jewish families, Orthodox Jewish families, all 25 families.
And the town was mostly farmers, the Gentiles and some of the Jews.
My family, the Kornreichs' two brothers had farms, both of them.
They were for many years in-- the Kornreichs-- in Tylicz.
And they worked land, and there was going from, from father to son, and then to grandson, and so forth.
And we still at the small farm.
Family, well, when I was growing up-- where do I start with the family?
Any time?
It was all together was seven children, OK?
And before World War I, a little girl was born that died as an infant, then was born another, another two girls two years apart, Gertrude and Zosia.
I knew exactly the, the year when it was, but I probably won't remember now.
I know she, she was 16 years older than I, you know, 16 years before me.
And Zosia was-- two years later, Zosia was born.
So World War I came.
There were two girls-- World War I-- Gertrude and Zosia.
And during World War I, two boys were born.

But I think it's the influenza, or whatever it was at this time there and, and-- it was all over the world, actually, influenza-- and the two boys died before there was a chance to get them some medication.
And then-- then 1920, after the war, two years, and I was born.
And two years later, 1922, my sister Danka was born.
My parents were Jewish Orthodox, very religious.
And my mother was born in Czechoslovakia, married very young, I think 17 or 18.
And my father was born in Tylicz and he was a [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know how to say it-- to yeshiva was going in Czechoslovakia.
And it was an arranged marriage.
And they were very happy, happy couple.
And it was a loving, lovely, wonderful family.
And all the 25 families, Jewish, lived with the Gentiles, the Ruthenians, and the Polish people in peace and harmony.
We didn't know ecumenical, the word ecumenical, what it meant, but we lived by it.
So we didn't-- I didn't know, really, much about anti-Semitism or, or any harsh, harsh things about, about it during the life when I was growing up.
And then the, the, the, the Greek Orthodox had their own school.
And the Polish school was for the Polish children.
And the Jewish children attended the Polish public schools, seven years.
And what else would you like?
We didn't have a rabbi in the town, but we had a shochet, which is a ritual slaughterer.
And he was-- [INAUDIBLE] he was studying to be a rabbi.

To-- he knew to [NON-ENGLISH], to say, you know-- that means, in, in Hebrew, [NON-ENGLISH] means-- in Jewish-- means that if there's something wrong with the animal that was slaughtered-- to give an example, on, on a chicken or a goose, the liver had spots or something, you would go to him asking is this kosher or not?
If it's not, then you throw it out.
So that was the shochet, and his name was Bleich.
And then there was a cheder.
There was a melamed, a teacher.
There were-- and in, in the Temple, in the, in the same room there, was, were the children studying every day from first grade, usually.
But I started to go to cheder.
They were only for boys.
But my mom insisted that she wants her daughter to study, to-- to study Hebrew, too.
So she talked to the Jewish community, and finally, after a lot of troubles, they allowed one girl.
So there's a big room, the Temple room, and there's a long table, and boys are sitting along benches on two sides of the table.
And on the other long table, one little girl is sitting studying.
And the rabbi was splitting himself between the studies for the boys, which they were studying Torah and everything else.

I was studying just Hebrew, to be able to read Hebrew and to translate a little bit, only like the prayers for the Sabbat or something, the cheder, as it's called, which-- siddur, siddur, which siddur is this week.
From their studying I listened a lot.
They were aloud practicing so I could-- I learned that, too.
But mostly it was writing and reading Hebrew.
Oh, and then my mother decided that he-- I'm sitting a lot of time while he was busy with the boys.
It was a lot longer.
And, and I'm not-- the boys are, are studying and practicing right there in the Temple.
Do you know any other language, she asked him, to teach my daughter?
And he says yes, I know German.
So he taught me German and Hebrew at the same time.

And while I was going to school, to the Polish school, the Polish public school decided so long as we have Ruthenians in town, the, the children, all the Polish and Jewish children should know the language, too, and alphabet.
So in first grade we started with the alphabet, Polish and Ruthenian.
And I had Hebrew and German, [LAUGHS] at seven years, four languages, and Jewish at home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it's four languages, and the fifth one was at home.
And the other languages I learned later.
Well, I don't know if you know about Hasidic Jews.
Everything is observed like the-- on the Sabbath not to do anything.
I mean, you fed the animals because that was necessary if you had a farm, like we did.
But no cooking.
So we had a big oven at the, at the shochet's-- at the rabbi's home.
And, and you brought a cholent you made.
You made it on Friday, and you brought it in the afternoon, late, to the, to the home there.
And his daughter [?
Golda ?] was waiting for us and put it all in the oven.
They were already prepared the oven with the coals went through it, and then the coals spread apart.
And by the next-- by Sabbat morning, they went all to [INAUDIBLE].
The little girls, the young girls didn't go.
So I didn't, only on holidays, on New Years, on Rosh Hashanah and, and Yom Kippur for a little while only.
And then on, on the Sabbat you would go over after the parents came home.
We would go over and bring the cholent home, and we had lunch.
And in the morning we had-- for, for coffee to warm up, which my mother prepared the day before-- we had a, a Gentile woman coming in.
And she would light the fire, and it was heating the coffee.
And during the war, we weren't allowed to have Gentile help, so we didn't have anything warm on Saturday morning.
The most of them-- or all of them as far as I know-- refused to, to light the fire themselves.
So they rather went without.
For fun, we played dominoes.
In the evening we played cards.
We-- on some-- the holidays and Purim, [? whatever ?] it was, we had a dreidel that we played for, for games to win.
You win if it's a Shin or, or whatever it is.
I don't know if you're familiar with it.
And reading, lots of reading.
So there was the school library, and there was my lay teacher.
As I said, one of them was a nun teaching, and one of them in the public school.
And one of them was a, a, a nun and one lay teacher.
And the lay teacher, Mrs.-- Miss [?
Bohinska ?] was her name.
She had a private library herself.
And so I, I was allowed-- some students were-- and I was, I was sort of a little bit helper of the teacher.
I helped to carry the books home.
And then I, I had the privilege to hang around a little bit longer and look in the library what [LAUGHS] books I wanted.
And she, she decided which one was suitable for me and which wasn't maybe.
I don't know.
But she said this one or this one.
If I picked it, she said, yes, that's historical romance.
It's OK.
Except my father didn't think it was OK.
[LAUGHS] So he took it away from me.
Said absolutely not.
It was history.
But in the history was a love story that I wasn't supposed to read.
So, so reading, a lot of reading with my sister and I and all the other children, too.
And then we played ball outside and throwing the ball, and walking, in the summer, mountain climbing and walking because we lived in the mountains like in North Carolina where I live now.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what else would you like to know?
Tell me about your social life as you were growing up.
Well, the social life was the 25 families.
There were weddings, bar mitzvahs, births, Pidyon ha-Bens, after the-- what's the first one called?
Can't think of the name when the circumcision takes place.
Pidyon ha-Ben is later, a month later, but the--
A bris, the bris and, and the Pidyon ha-Ben later on, I think it's a month later.
And all the families-- there were never any invitations sent away.
Everybody attended everybody's [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what [NON-ENGLISH] is?
So the holiday, anything that we celebrated, all of us celebrated together.
So this was a lot of fun, too.
By Purim, for example, for that holiday, we were-- everybody was baking the best cookies that they knew how.
And [NON-ENGLISH], it's called, from one house to the other one.

So usually the little children, either a boy or a girl-- we didn't have boys, so the girls were doing-- a plate with your own a home-baked cookies and covered with a nice doily, and you were bringing it to all the people, wherever Mom decided to go to bring it.
And they, they gave the children some money.
So you ended up with a pocket full of money after, [LAUGHS] after this.
And Mom baked the cookies for it.
And every family to other families, so they had a lot of cookies from everybody else.
And they had, for months probably, cookies there.
And what else did we do for fun?
As I said, walking, mountain climbing, and some of them that knew how, they were going swimming, too, mostly the boys.
But there was a swimming pool in that little town nearby that there was mixed swimming.
So I wasn't allowed.
So I don't know how to swim to this day.
And we [? didn't-- ?] Hasidic Jews, the children, the girls weren't allowed in mixed swimming pools, so we didn't learn to swim.
But the boys could swim at all time.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And fishing, that's right.
Yeah, I forgot about that.
Most of the boys were fishing for the Shabbos for fish to have on Friday night to have the fish and to have it for the Sabbat.
And there wasn't always enough money.
We are not always had the chance to go to another town five kilometers from our-- seven kilometers, five miles, approximately between four and five miles to a town to buy the fish.
So we were all fishing.
The boys were fishing to have-- and we didn't have any boys.
So I, I wanted to go and bring the fish in, too.
And I had to pay my way in.
My mother was baking Friday morning-- usually, [INAUDIBLE]-- my mother was baking a cheese danish in the morning.
If I had cheese danish with me and had for the two of 'em that they were taking me along-- because they weren't taking girls along, because girls talk and they scare the fish away.
So I was paying my way in with the two cheese danish to bring from my mom.
And they loved it.
So I was allowed.
I don't think too many girls were allowed, a few of other ones, too.
But you had to buy your way in to go for the-- and without the cheese danish, I don't think they would have let me.
And I-- whitefish usually, some of them maybe this size-- what would you say-- about seven inches, even those.
And that's brought home and Mom prepared them in a sweet and sour sauce with raisins and something like this.
So we had a lot to do, you see, all, all healthy things to do.
And you know something?
Whatever we had to do, we were never bored.
And there was work around the house, too, that we had to help.
We were never bored.
And we didn't know about all the luxuries that the world had.
So we weren't missing anything.
Very happy childhoods from all of us, as far as I know.
How were the relations between the Jewish community and the non-Jewish community?
Just wonderful, there was absolutely never-- I don't remember.
At one time I remember without anti-Semitic remark or something, that something happened.
I was the first Jewish girl that was given by the teacher-- as I said, I was sort of the favorite for the lay teacher when I was in fourth grade.
The first one-- the first, second, and third, I was where the nun was teaching.
But in the fourth grade, when I was-- she gave me the privilege to be the school sales lady for the books, books and, and pencils what children needed, and helping her bring the, the books home.
And what was the main thing I wanted to tell you?
I almost forgot.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, yes, and she decided that Jewish children usually spoke Jewish at home.
And our older sister, Zosia, was living with us.
Gertrude was already the first one, that I said, the oldest one alive.
The first child died, and the two boys.
The oldest one emigrated to America in the same year that I was born.
The picture in the book shows me about eight or nine months old.
This was the [INAUDIBLE] picture from her to the family.
And she lived, and she lived in New York City.
And uncle, my mom's brother, took-- sent the papers, the affidavits for her.
And she went to America when she was 17.
She's 16 years old, and I was almost a year old.
So she went to the United States.
So Zosia was at home.
And Zosia decided when we came home from school, every time we spoke, the three of us together-- Zosia the oldest one-- Zosia was 14 years older and Gertrude 16 from me.
And so when Danka and I came home from school, we were speaking Polish with her.
So it ended up that we spoke pretty good Polish, and we did a lot of reading, which maybe other children didn't.
So the teacher decided that I was good enough in the Polish language and she said I was a pretty good talker, too.
I, I think that's why I got the-- I got a speech from the national holiday which was the President Marshall Pilsudski .
He was the liberator what fought for liberating Poland from, from the Russians, from Poland occupation, and a hero, a Polish hero.
And on the-- because of the nation being 90% Catholic, they celebrated not his birthday when he was born, but his saint day.
His name was Jozef, Joseph, and on March 19, I think, is the St.
Joseph Day.
There was a big celebration at front of city hall, all the dignitaries sitting on two benches on a veranda, and then the ones who had speeches would come up and stand next to a table.
I was so short that they had to put me on a little stool to address the public.
And then the public means everybody in town-- because everybody had children in school-- assembled outside, the Jewish families and other ones, listening to the speeches and to the songs.
That teacher that I said, the lay teacher, she played beautifully the violin.
So the songs-- there was first speeches.
And I had the first, and I think the last Jewish girl that had a speech, and she-- I think it was because of the language, that I mastered the Polish language better than the other ones.
Or maybe she thought I was a good speaker.
I don't know.
But I got this speech and nobody after me after I finished grade school.
And then there was the singing.
The children assembled are singing, but when it came to the singing, I've a terrible voice.
She took me from the first row to the back, and she said move your lips.
So that's all I did.
[LAUGHS] And I was never mad at her, because she was so nice anyway and gave me so many other privileges.
In fact, I had a privilege because in school there was one-- oh, yes, before-- the reason I told you about the speech was because I wanna tell you-- one of the girls was jealous, a Gentile girl.
That's the only time I experienced-- [?
Janka Migresh ?] was her name.
And she stole the speech from me.
I took it to school.
[INAUDIBLE] And this was when the girls were-- everybody was going out.
I practiced reading and so I will know it by heart.
Didn't have the speech in front of me.
I, I had to learn it by, by heart, the teacher said.
And, and she stole it from me, and the teacher made me another copy.
And this was the only experience that I had, otherwise we didn't.
And then, there was a boy, a really-- he was three years in the same grade, or something.
He wasn't very smart.
But he was mean.
And there was [?
Moishe Goldfinger ?], and, and every time he picked on him, kind of a boy that is timid and soft spoken.
And he picked this one.
The bully picked on this boy and pulled his payos.
In the third and the fourth, fourth grade, I didn't do anything about it, but it bothered me terribly, because [?
Moishe ?] was shaking.
And I didn't-- couldn't stand the injustice of a bully.
Then one day when I was in fifth grade, I waited there, [? where ?] I knew he's meet him at during recess.
And after he pulled his payos, I punched him in the nose.
And then I went right back to [? school. ?] I had to jump up.
For ?] I went to school.
And he came in later, about five minutes later with a bloody nose.
And the teacher said what happened to you?
He says Rena punched me in the nose.
She, she turned away to the side.
I could see her.
I was grinning first.
She couldn't.
She wanted to laugh so hard.
She was grinning a little bit.
She says, you must have deserved it if Rena did it.
[LAUGHS] Can I have a little bit of water?
[DRINKS] So this is about all the anti-Semitism again.
My parents with the other neighbors, if we needed something, if we didn't have-- we had a, a woman that was taking the cows out, the cows out to pasture.
And if we didn't have that woman or was sick, somebody else would take over.
And really very nice life.
Even-- even when-- in-- on September 1st, 1939, Hitler's armies marched in, they were forbidden to make any deals with Jews.
Borrowing a cup of sugar would have been a deal.
And they didn't listen to it.
And they risked their life, sort of, with still living nicely and being nice and kind-- some of them, almost most of them.
There was one that didn't act so nicely that it isn't even worth mentioning.
Everything that the public school teaches, no religion in school.
The children had on Wednesday afternoon, the Catholic children had on Wednesday afternoon religion.
And the Jewish children could leave, had the privilege to leave the school.
And on Saturday there were classes, too, half a day, because Wednesday was a half a day.
And that's why Saturday they had a half a day of school.
And the Jewish children were free to stay home because of the Sabbath.
We didn't write on the Sabbath, and we couldn't carry things on the Sabbath because that was a small town.
There were no telephone wires around.
It has to be closed in, in order to carry something, even a handkerchief.
The Hasidic Orthodox Jews' rules were that you don't carry.
If you wanted a handkerchief, you tied it around your wrist to wear it, and no carrying no money or anything.
So we could-- we didn't-- so the homework, the teacher was so nice, all the teachers, the nun, too, that there was never given on Saturday.
Always the homework for the weekend was given on Friday so the Jewish children could do the homework on Sunday before we came to school.
So it couldn't be any, any nicer.
Just wonderful memories from it, that we lived in peace.
And, and during the war, as I said, they couldn't deal with Jews.
But if you-- they allowed us, if we were working, handwork, doing something for a Gentile, then we could get something for it, either money or, or food for it.
And, and my older sister Zosia escaped during--
While later on, after the, the Germans came in and they forbid us all proclamations for the-- no-- no going to-- we couldn't go to a doctor.
They took away all the privileges from us that free people have, going to a doctor, seeing a dentist.
You couldn't.
We couldn't go from one town to the other one visiting-- visiting somebody.
And we couldn't earn a living.
And we couldn't-- with the farmers.
And the stores, I think, were still open.
The kosher butcher was still open.
But we couldn't have-- later on, they forbid that, too.
We couldn't have-- go buy in-- for money in the store.
But if I made-- if you made something or did something for the Gentile-- so my sister Zosia was-- escaped with a little boy.
He needed tonsils removed.
It was already scheduled at the doctor, and they wouldn't allow it.
So he had a guy, like I had later, and they brought her to Czechoslovakia.
And she had it done, the surgery done.
And she remained in Czechoslovakia.
She had two children at that time.
OK, I want to stay back before the war.
I wanted to ask you when you weren't in school, summers for example, did you have any jobs that you did?
Or--
Not in grade school.
After grade school, after grade school, I went to extended education courses they were offering because there was no official high school there-- or gymnasium it's called there.
Then the, the teachers, the local teachers, arranged from all the schools, from the two schools, that thought if somebody-- there were some people didn't know how to read and write.
So that was for the elderly who didn't know how to read and write.
It was for the one who wanted to advance.
Like I finished the seventh grade.
And my teacher kept me for one more year, to the eighth.
So I was a little bit-- 14-- older than the other children.
And then she kept me for helping to do-- to-- to correct the homework from the smaller, the lower classes.
And then she and the other teachers arranged it that from other towns [INAUDIBLE] teachers came in and taught during the whole year for extended education.
And I-- I had already studied the German, so I didn't take the German there if they were offering it.
And also the subjects what you teach in extended education after grade school.
But most of the children were just, just in grade, grade school education.
Oh, yes.
We, we didn't have an [INAUDIBLE] newspaper.
But in Krynica-- as I said, seven kilometers from us, was a world-famous resort.
It's Krynica.
There were villas, there was all the luxury that-- that anybody can imagine.
You could hear on the course while walking-- could hear five, six languages, foreign languages speaking.
People from all over the world used to come to the mountains for the winter for skiing, and the summer for mineral baths they had there.
So [INAUDIBLE]-- so this was also part of the children when we were old enough, after grade school, to take a walk the seven kilometers to Krynica.
We could go to Krynica, and we could listen to famous singers.
Jan Kiepura was a Polish famous singer, Marta Eggerth.
You probably don't know the name.
But they were very little-known here in America, even though he died and lived the last years of his life-- when the Germans came, they escaped to America, he and his wife.
And Marta Eggerth and-- both singers and-- so we would go to a famous singer like this one, Jan Kiepura.
And on the deck there were benches there and you had to pay something to go and listen to the, to the concert.
And he dedicated it-- every Wednesday that was in the fall usually-- every Wednesday he dedicated all of the money to the poor people.
So we were allowed to go to Krynica, for example.
And we could-- we didn't have a radio, and we didn't have a gramophone, so there wasn't much music.
So this music-- and why we go, we went to Krynica, they played, they played music there, there, too, and in one of the places where the mineral water was.
Can you spell that for me, Krynica?
Krynica, K-R-Y-N-I-C-A.
You would pronounce it 'Krynicka.' But the c is pronounced in Polish as 'Krynitsa.'
OK.
